content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 100v–100r | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_002_page_001.jpg, DEU_002_page_002.jpg; source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/DEU_002_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_002_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

**1** "Then we turned around and set out into the wilderness on the road to the Red Sea, as the LORD had told me. And we wandered for a long time around the hill country of Seir.

**2** "Finally the LORD said to me: **3** 'You have been going around this hill country long enough. Turn north. **4** Command the people: You are about to pass through the territory of your relatives, the descendants of Esau who live in Seir. They will be afraid of you, but you must be very careful. **5** Do not provoke them to war, for I will not give you so much as a single foot of their land — because I have given the hill country of Seir to Esau as his own. **6** You will purchase food from them with silver so you can eat, and you will also buy water from them with silver so you can drink. **7** For the LORD your God has blessed everything you have done. He has watched over your journey through this vast wilderness. For forty years the LORD your God has been with you, and you have lacked nothing.'

**8** "So we passed through the territory of our brothers, the descendants of Esau who live in Seir, and we left the Arabah road that runs from Elath and Ezion-geber. We turned and went along the road to the wilderness of Moab.

**9** "The LORD told me: 'Do not trouble Moab or provoke them to war, for I will not give you any of their land. I have given Ar as a possession to the sons of Lot.' **10** (The Emim lived there formerly — a great and numerous people, tall as the Anakim. **11** Like the Anakim they were also classified as Rephaim, though the Moabites called them Emim. **12** The Horites formerly lived in Seir, but the descendants of Esau displaced them, wiping them out and settling in their place — just as Israel did in the land the LORD gave them as a possession.)

**13** "'Now get up and cross the Wadi Zered.' So we crossed the Wadi Zered. **14** The time we had traveled from Kadesh-barnea until we crossed the Wadi Zered was thirty-eight years — until the entire generation of fighting men had perished from the camp, as the LORD had sworn concerning them. **15** Indeed, the LORD's hand was against them, to root them out from the camp until they were gone.

**16** "Once all the fighting men among the people had died, **17** the LORD said to me: **18** 'Today you are to cross the border of Moab, past Ar. **19** When you come near the Ammonites, do not trouble them or pick a fight with them, for I will not give you any of the land of the Ammonites — I have given it as a possession to the sons of Lot.' **20** (That land too was considered the territory of the Rephaim — the Rephaim formerly lived there, and the Ammonites called them Zamzummim. **21** They were a great and numerous people, tall as the Anakim. But the LORD destroyed them before the Ammonites, and they displaced them and settled in their place. **22** The LORD did the same for the descendants of Esau who live in Seir, when he destroyed the Horites before them so they could dispossess them and settle in their place, as they have done to this day. **23** As for the Avvim who lived in villages as far as Gaza — the Caphtorim, who came from Caphtor, destroyed them and settled in their place.)

**24** "'Set out now — break camp and cross the Arnon Gorge. Look — I am handing over to you Sihon the Amorite king of Heshbon, together with his land. Begin to take possession of it; wage war against him. **25** This very day I will begin to put the dread and fear of you on all the nations under heaven. When they hear reports of you, they will tremble and writhe in anguish before you.'

**26** "So I sent messengers from the wilderness of Kedemoth to Sihon king of Heshbon with a peaceful offer, saying: **27** 'Let me pass through your land. I will travel only on the highway — I will not turn aside to the right or to the left. **28** You may sell me food to eat and water to drink for silver. All I ask is that you let me pass through on foot — **29** just as the descendants of Esau who live in Seir did for me, and the Moabites who live in Ar — until I cross the Jordan into the land the LORD our God is giving us.' **30** But Sihon king of Heshbon refused to let us pass, because the LORD your God had made him stubborn and hardened his heart, in order to hand him over to you as he has done today.

**31** "The LORD said to me: 'See — I have begun to deliver Sihon and his land to you. Begin to take possession of his land.' **32** Sihon came out against us with all his forces to do battle at Jahaz. **33** The LORD our God handed him over to us, and we struck him down along with his sons and all his fighting men. **34** At that time we captured all his cities and completely destroyed every city — men, women, and children. We left no survivors. **35** We took only the livestock as plunder for ourselves, along with the spoil from the cities we had captured. **36** From Aroer on the edge of the Arnon Gorge — including the town in the gorge — all the way to Gilead, there was not a single city too well-fortified for us. The LORD our God gave them all to us. **37** But you did not encroach on the land of the Ammonites — neither the banks of the Jabbok, nor the cities of the hill country, nor anywhere else the LORD our God had forbidden."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Dynamic choices — PNEUMA:**

**V.3 — "You have been going around this hill country long enough":** The Hebrew rav lakhem sov et ha-har hazeh — "it is enough for you to circle this mountain" — carries weariness and also divine determination. PNEUMA stretches this into natural English while keeping the finality of God's new direction.

**V.5 — "not so much as a single foot of their land":** Hebrew ad midras kaph ragel — literally "as far as the tread of a foot." PNEUMA renders the idiom into forceful colloquial English to capture the absolute prohibition.

**V.7 — "He has watched over your journey":** Hebrew yada YHWH elohekha — literally "the LORD your God has known your walking." "Known" here means intimate, attentive care. PNEUMA renders "watched over" to convey the relational warmth.

**V.14 — "The time we had traveled...thirty-eight years":** Moses' accounting of the tragic years carries quiet grief. PNEUMA preserves the arithmetic without embellishment — the numbers themselves bear the weight.

**V.15 — "The LORD's hand was against them":** A stark reversal: the same hand that protected them now dismantles them. PNEUMA keeps this unambiguous — divine judgment expressed as direct agency.

**V.25 — "they will tremble and writhe in anguish":** Hebrew ragazu ve-chalu — to shake and be in labor-pain. The nations are depicted in physical distress. PNEUMA uses "tremble and writhe" to convey both the fear and the bodily dimension.

**V.30 — "the LORD your God had made him stubborn and hardened his heart":** The theology here is deliberate: as with Pharaoh in Exodus, God hardens Sihon so that the victory will be total and undeniable. PNEUMA does not soften this — it is the text's own claim and must be rendered clearly.

**Vaticanus (LXX) observation:** Vaticanus page 144 carries the later portion of Chapter 1 and opens Chapter 2. The Greek at v.7 reads "oiden kyrios ho theos sou" — "the LORD your God has known" — a more literal rendering than typical dynamic translations. At v.14 the LXX renders "thirty-eight years" identically to the MT. At v.30 the Vaticanus reads "eskleryne kyrios ho theos sou to pneuma autou" — "the LORD your God hardened his spirit" — where the MT has "hiqshah YHWH elohekha et-rucho." The Vaticanus uses "pneuma" (spirit) for "ruach" (wind/spirit/breath) — a consistent rendering that links directly to the PNEUMA translation's own name and commitments.
